questions.scenario: DevPolaris routes public HTTPS traffic to an ALB on port 443, forwards requests from the ALB to ECS tasks on port 3000, and lets those tasks reach RDS on port 5432. After a security-group cleanup, target health reports `Target.Timeout`; the task security group allows port 3000 only from `sg-developer-vpn`. Which rule is missing from the intended request path?

options.text: Allow the ALB security group on task port 3000 but leave the task inbound rule restricted to `sg-developer-vpn`.
Task SG inbound TCP 3000 from `sg-orders-alb`.
Allow task inbound TCP 3000 from the VPC CIDR so health checks work even if the ALB security group changes.
Allow ALB outbound TCP 3000 to the task subnet CIDR but leave task inbound limited to the developer VPN.

questions.explanation:
The ALB is the caller for the app port, so the task security group must allow port `3000` from the ALB security group. A developer VPN rule may let one engineer test, but it does not prove the real caller can reach the task. IAM does not forward packets between ALB and ECS.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
During a rollout, old tasks are replaced with new task IPs:

Old task is 10.20.10.45. New task is 10.20.11.72. Security group is sg-orders-task.

Why does the DB rule `TCP 5432 from sg-orders-task` age better than `TCP 5432 from 10.20.10.45/32`?

options.text: The security-group reference follows network interfaces assigned that group, while a `/32` follows only one replaced address.
A subnet CIDR source would also survive task replacement but would admit unrelated resources in that subnet.
The new task keeps the intended network role when it is associated with `sg-orders-task`.
A service-managed prefix list would identify the ECS service more precisely than the task security group.

questions.explanation:
Fargate task IPs are replaceable, so a single-IP rule is brittle. The security group reference follows the role of the task tier as tasks are replaced. That is exactly why resource-level rules are clearer inside an AWS app path.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
A database subnet NACL is evaluated in rule-number order:

100 inbound deny TCP 5432 from 10.20.10.0/24 120 inbound allow TCP 5432 from 10.20.0.0/16 32767 inbound deny all

The orders API task is `10.20.10.45`. What happens to its database request?

options.text: Treat the stateful security-group allow as overriding the lower-numbered NACL deny.
Rule 120 allows the packet because NACLs use the most specific CIDR rather than rule order.
It is denied by rule 100 before the later broader allow rule is considered.
The result depends on the outbound NACL only because inbound rules do not apply to database listener traffic.

questions.explanation:
NACL rules are checked from the lowest rule number upward. A matching deny at rule 100 stops the packet before the allow at rule 120 can help. This is why rule order matters when NACLs become stricter.

Learn more in [VPC network ACLs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html).

questions.scenario: A production network review must preserve the browser-to-ALB-to-task-to-database request path while narrowing unnecessary access. Which review actions improve the security group design without breaking the intended orders path?

options.text: Use `sg-orders-task` as the source for DB inbound TCP 5432.
Allow DB inbound TCP 5432 from both `sg-orders-task` and the whole VPC CIDR as a temporary fallback with no expiry.
Use `sg-orders-alb` as the source for task inbound TCP 3000.
Keep ALB inbound TCP 443 from public clients because it is the public front door.
Allow task inbound TCP 3000 from the public client CIDRs in addition to the ALB security group.

questions.explanation:
The intended path is internet to ALB, ALB to task, and task to database. Security group references express the internal hops cleanly, while the ALB keeps the narrow public entry on HTTPS. A broad VPC CIDR on every rule erases useful boundaries.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
devpolaris-orders-api starts successfully, reads its database secret, then logs:

```text
error=database_connect_failed
host=orders-staging.abcxyz.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com
port=5432
cause=connect ETIMEDOUT 10.20.41.18:5432
```

Which layer should you inspect first?

options.text: The task role permission for `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue`.
Network rules and routing for the task-to-RDS packet path.
The Route 53 public alias for `orders.devpolaris.com`.
The ACM certificate attached to the HTTPS listener.

questions.explanation:
The task already read the secret, so this is not the first IAM check. The timeout names a host, port, and private IP, which points to packet reachability. Start with the RDS security group, task security group, routes, and NACLs if needed.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
Two database rule proposals are on the review:

Proposal A is TCP 5432 from 10.20.0.0/16. Proposal B is TCP 5432 from sg-orders-task.

The database only serves devpolaris-orders-api. Which proposal is better and why?

options.text: Proposal A is easier to operate because it continues working when another application is placed in the VPC.
Proposal B is narrower because only network interfaces associated with `sg-orders-task` can use the rule as the source.
Proposal B is clearer, but Proposal A may be required if the path crosses a middlebox that prevents security-group referencing.
Proposal B, because it describes the app task tier as the sender.

questions.explanation:
A security group reference describes the role in the architecture with a named source group. That is helpful for Fargate tasks whose private IPs can change. It also avoids giving unrelated VPC resources the same chance to reach the database port.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
A flow-log style record appears after the same change:

srcaddr=10.20.12.44 dstaddr=10.20.2.18 srcport=3000 dstport=51748 protocol=6 action=REJECT

The target group reports `Target.Timeout`. What does the rejected packet most likely represent?

options.text: Return traffic from the app target port to an ephemeral client-side port.
A browser attempting to connect directly to the RDS database port.
A Route 53 resolver query being blocked by an ACM validation record.
An IAM policy evaluation being denied by the ECS task role.

questions.explanation:
The source port is `3000`, which matches the app target port. The destination port is a high temporary port, which looks like return traffic. With NACLs, that return path must be explicitly allowed.

Learn more in [VPC network ACLs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html).
